preamble: The voiced palatal lateral approximant /ʎ/ can undergo a phonetic transformation to the voiceless postalveolar fricative /ʃ/ through a series of sound changes. This shift typically involves delateralization, spirantization, and devoicing, and is attested in the historical development of certain Romance languages.
phoneticEffects: This transformation involves several phonetic changes: delateralization (loss of lateral articulation), spirantization (change from approximant to fricative), devoicing (loss of vocal fold vibration), and a slight retraction of the place of articulation from palatal to postalveolar

examples.note: The Latin /lj/ sequence developed into /ʎ/, which then underwent spirantization and devoicing to /ʃ/, and subsequently to /x/ in the evolution of Spanish. The form *mugier* represents an earlier stage with /ʃ/ before the shift to /x/.
In Rioplatense Spanish, the historical /ʎ/ (or its merger with /ʝ/) is realized as a voiceless postalveolar fricative [ʃ], a phenomenon known as *ʃeísmo*. For example, *llave* [ˈʎaβe] > [ˈʃaβe].

sources: Zampaulo, A. (2012). When Synchrony Meets Diachrony: (Alveolo)Palatal Sound Patterns In Spanish and Other Romance Languages. The Ohio State University.
Book Review of Zampaulo, André. Palatal Sound Changes in the Romance Languages. Diachronic and Synchronic Perspectives. (2014). Romance Philology, 68(1), 163-168.
